Description: Examines Go source code and reports suspicious constructs that might be bugs such as unreachable code or bad format strings
When To Use: Run before every commit and in CI to catch common mistakes early
Output: Text report listing file:line and description of each suspicious construct found

Description: Meta-linter that runs dozens of linters in parallel with caching and provides a unified report
When To Use: Use in CI and pre-commit hooks for comprehensive code quality checks
Output: Unified report from multiple linters with file locations and severity levels

Description: Formats Go source code according to the canonical Go style by adjusting whitespace and alignment
When To Use: Run on save or pre-commit to maintain consistent formatting across the codebase
Output: Reformatted source files written in-place; diff output with -d flag

Description: Stricter superset of gofmt that enforces additional formatting rules like removing unnecessary blank lines
When To Use: Use as a drop-in replacement for gofmt when you want stricter formatting consistency
Output: Reformatted source files with stricter rules applied; compatible with gofmt output

Description: Enables the built-in race detector to find data races by instrumenting memory accesses during test execution
When To Use: Run regularly in CI and during development when working with concurrent code
Output: Reports data race locations with goroutine stacks showing conflicting accesses

Description: Runs benchmark functions matching the regex and reports ns/op and allocations per operation
When To Use: Use when optimizing performance-critical code or comparing implementations
Output: Benchmark name followed by iterations and ns/op and B/op and allocs/op metrics

Description: Runs fuzz tests that generate random inputs to find crashes and edge cases in your code
When To Use: Use to discover edge cases in parsing functions and input validation code
Output: Reports crashing inputs saved to testdata/fuzz/ corpus directory for regression

Description: Measures statement-level test coverage and reports the percentage of code exercised by tests
When To Use: Run to check test coverage meets your project minimum threshold
Output: Percentage of statements covered per package printed to stdout

Description: Generates an interactive HTML report showing covered and uncovered code highlighted in green and red
Command: go test -coverprofile=coverage.out ./... && go tool cover -html=coverage.out -o coverage.html
When To Use: Use to visually identify untested code paths and find coverage gaps
Output: HTML file with color-coded source showing covered lines in green and uncovered in red

Description: Analyzes CPU and memory profiles to find performance bottlenecks and memory allocation hotspots
Command: go tool pprof -http=:8080 cpu.prof, or go tool pprof http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/heap
When To Use: Use when diagnosing performance issues or high memory usage in production or development
Output: Interactive web UI with flame graphs and top functions sorted by resource usage

Description: Visualizes execution traces showing goroutine scheduling and syscalls and network blocking over time
Command: go test -trace=trace.out && go tool trace trace.out
When To Use: Use to diagnose goroutine scheduling issues and latency problems and understand concurrency behavior
Output: Interactive web UI with timeline showing goroutine states and events and regions

Description: Full-featured Go debugger supporting breakpoints and stepping and variable inspection and goroutine switching
When To Use: Use when print-debugging is insufficient and you need to step through code or inspect state interactively
Output: Interactive REPL or DAP server for IDE integration with breakpoints and variable watches

Description: Runs commands described by //go:generate directives in Go source files to automate code generation
When To Use: Use to generate mocks and string methods and protobuf code and other derived source files
Output: Generated .go files written to disk as specified by the generator tool directives

Description: Injects values into variables at build time via the linker for version info and build metadata
Command: go build -ldflags "-X main.version=1.2.3 -X main.commit=$(git rev-parse HEAD)" ./cmd/app
When To Use: Use to embed version and commit hash and build time into your binary without source changes
Output: Binary compiled with injected variable values accessible at runtime

Description: Adds missing module dependencies and removes unused ones from go.mod and go.sum
When To Use: Run after adding or removing imports and before committing to keep go.mod and go.sum clean
Output: Updated go.mod and go.sum files with only the dependencies actually used

Description: Copies all module dependencies into a vendor/ directory for offline and reproducible builds
When To Use: Use when you need reproducible builds or must work in environments without network access
Output: vendor/ directory containing copies of all dependent module source code

Description: Manages multi-module workspaces for simultaneous development of related modules without replace directives
Command: go work init ./module-a ./module-b, then go work sync
When To Use: Use when developing multiple interdependent modules locally in a monorepo or side-by-side
Output: go.work file defining workspace modules; all modules resolved locally during development

Description: Scans your dependencies for known vulnerabilities using the Go vulnerability database
When To Use: Run in CI and before releases to ensure no known vulnerabilities exist in your dependency tree
Output: List of vulnerabilities with CVE IDs and affected functions and recommended fix versions

Description: Advanced static analyzer that finds bugs and suggests simplifications and flags deprecated API usage
When To Use: Run alongside go vet in CI for deeper analysis including unused code and deprecated API detection
Output: Text report with check codes like SA1000 and file locations and descriptions of issues

Description: Live-reloading development server that watches for file changes and automatically rebuilds and restarts your app
When To Use: Use during local development for rapid iteration without manually restarting the server
Output: Automatic rebuild and restart on file change with colorized build output in terminal

Description: All-in-one protobuf tooling for linting and breaking change detection and code generation
Command: buf lint, buf breaking, buf generate
When To Use: Use when working with Protocol Buffers and gRPC to enforce schema quality and detect breaking changes
Output: Lint violations and breaking change reports and generated Go and gRPC code from proto files

Description: Automates building Go binaries and Docker images and creating changelogs and publishing releases
When To Use: Use to automate cross-platform binary builds and GitHub/GitLab releases with changelogs
Output: Cross-compiled binaries and Docker images and checksums and changelogs published to release platforms
